sections.heading: Core Concept
sections.content: A linked list is like a treasure hunt — each clue points to the next location. It is built from a series of **nodes** chained together via **pointers**, linking data scattered across memory into a highly flexible [[primary|dynamic data structure]].
items: Each **node** contains a **data field** that stores a value and a **pointer field** that points to the next address — the [[primary|building block]] of the list
The first node is called the **Head**; the last node's **pointer** points to null, marking the [[primary|end of the sequence]]
A singly linked list only moves forward; a doubly linked list adds **prev**, allowing backward traversal
Because nodes are scattered randomly in memory, the list does not support [[primary|random access]] — you must **traverse** from the head to reach any specific element

sections.heading: Operation Complexity
sections.content: Performance depends on pointer mechanics. When estimating operation cost, ask: **Do we already have the target node, or do we need to traverse first?**
items: **Read and search**: [[warning|O(n)]]. No random access — must traverse sequentially from the head to find the target
**Head insert/delete**: [[success|O(1)]]. Only the head pointer changes; no other nodes are affected
**Tail insert**: [[success|O(1)]] with a **tail pointer**; [[warning|O(n)]] without one, because traversal to the end is required
**Insert/delete at a known position**: [[success|O(1)]]. Only adjacent node pointers need updating — no element shifting

sections.heading: What It Optimizes
sections.content: It excels at **frequent insertions and deletions** and data whose size is unknown in advance. It is not well-suited for **random access** or **frequent searches** — use an **array** instead when read performance matters.
items: **Frequent node insert/delete**: especially efficient at the head and tail, making it ideal for implementing **stacks** and **queues**
**Dynamically changing data size**: no need to pre-allocate contiguous memory — the list grows and shrinks on demand
**Not suited for random access**: without an index mechanism, reaching a specific position requires traversal from the head
**Memory-constrained scenarios**: each node needs extra space to store its **pointer**, which increases total memory overhead

sections.heading: When To Use It
sections.content: A linked list is a strong signal when the problem involves frequent head/tail insertions and deletions, cannot rely on index-based random access, or requires merging, splitting, or reversing dynamic sequences.
items: **Reverse a linked list**: flip the order of an existing sequence (e.g. 1->2->3 becomes 3->2->1) — a pure test of pointer redirection and temporary storage
**Find the middle or k-th-from-end node**: locate the midpoint or N-th-from-last element without knowing the total length — the classic use case for the fast-and-slow pointer technique
**Merge sorted lists**: interleave two sorted sequences by rewiring pointers alone — no data movement needed
**Odd-even node regrouping**: split and reconnect nodes by position parity — tests the ability to maintain and update multiple pointer chains simultaneously

sections.heading: Pointer Pitfalls
sections.content: Beginners often break the entire list by **reversing the pointer assignment order**, causing nodes to be lost permanently. Forgetting to handle an **empty list** or **head/tail boundaries** leads to [[danger|Null Dereference]] crashes — the most common stumbling block in implementation.
items: **Reversed assignment order**: when inserting, modifying the original pointer before saving the new node's pointer causes all subsequent nodes to [[danger|be lost forever]]
**Ignoring null pointer exceptions**: accessing a pointer's property without first checking for an **empty list** causes [[danger|a program crash]]
**Missing head/tail boundary updates**: during head or tail operations, forgetting to sync the **Head** or **Tail** variable, or not setting the tail's pointer to **Null**
**Incorrect deletion**: removing a node before reconnecting its neighbors creates an orphan node that is unreachable

sections.heading: Array vs Linked List
sections.content: Beginners are often anchored to the [[primary|contiguous memory]] mindset of arrays. Comparing the fundamental difference in how the two structures allocate memory helps you switch mental models faster and understand why **pointers** are necessary.
items: Arrays occupy a fixed block of **contiguous memory**; linked list nodes are scattered randomly and can [[success|grow or shrink]] dynamically
Arrays support [[success|O(1)]] random access by index; linked lists must follow **pointers** node by node, taking [[warning|O(n)]]
Arrays require shifting many elements on insert/delete; linked lists only rewire neighboring **pointers**, so insert/delete is [[success|O(1)]]
